feed.subtitle: SAP HANA Cloud blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Unlocking custom AI use-cases with SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
If you’re working with data in SAP landscapes, you already know the pressure to turn it into real outcomes.  The good news.  You don’t need to move your data or add complexity to start building AI use cases.

In this upcoming session, we’ll walk through how to use the built-in AI capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud to create practical, scalable solutions.  From AutoML with PAL to new Data and Discovery Agents, you’ll see how to go from raw data to actionable insights in a structured way.

Plus, we’ll share an early look at Tabular AI with SAP-RPT-1.  This is where things start to get interesting for partners building next-gen applications.

The focus is simple.  Clear steps.  Real examples.  Immediate value.

Join us and leave with a plan you can apply right away.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013362.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AI #DataAnalytics #MachineLearning

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Hana Cloud
entry.content:
As a continuation of the series on the Introductory Guide to Provisioning SAP Business Data Cloud and its various components, this blog will help you provision SAP HANA Cloud within Business Data Cloud. Please refer to the different parts to get a holistic understanding of the entire process.

Part 1: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud through SAP4Me
Part 2: Provisioning of SAP Datasphere
Part 3: Provisioning of SAP Analytics Cloud
Part 4: Provisioning of SAP Databricks
Part 5: Installation of Intelligent Apps and Data Packages
Part 6: Provisioning of SAP HANA Cloud

#Part 6
In this blog, we will discuss in detail how to provision SAP HANA Cloud via Business Data Cloud Core.

Prerequisites

  • An active SAP Business Data Cloud subscription with a valid Entitlement System ID.
  • Access to SAP for Me (me.sap.com) with the appropriate provisioning roles.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud Core must be enabled — you will see the confirmation banner on the Applications tab.
  • A configured SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS) tenant is available in your landscape.
  • Sufficient remaining Capacity Units in your contract to allocate to the new solution.
Note: The provisioning wizard is a guided, multi-step flow. Each step must be completed in sequence. You can navigate back using the Previous Step button to revise entries before finalising
 
Provisioning SAP HANA Cloud creates a single SAP HANA database instance within an SAP-managed application tenant. When the HANA database instance is provisioned, the SAP HANA Cloud tools are automatically set up as well. These tools, accessible from the SAP HANA Cloud tenant in SAP for Me, allow you to:

  • Manage the database instance (see Managing SAP HANA Cloud Instances), including resizing, adding or removing replicas, and creating ECNs.

  • Work with the database using SAP HANA Database Explorer (see Getting Started with SAP HANA Database Explorer).

  • Monitor the instance’s capacity unit consumption (see Consumption Monitor in SAP HANA Cloud Central).


Step-by-Step Provisioning Guide

Log in to SAP for Me nd navigate to Portfolio & Products > Business Data Cloud. On the Applications tab you will find all BDC applications that are available for provisioning within your entitlement, including:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP Databricks
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP Datasphere, Data Composer
  • SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP Business Data Cloud Connect
  1. A green confirmation banner confirms that SAP Business Data Cloud Core is enabled, and provisioning can begin. Click Start Provisioning on the SAP HANA Cloud tile

     

  2. In the "Provision SAP HANA Cloud" dialog that appears, provide details such as Solution Name, Path and Business Type.  The Business Type available by default is 'Production’


  3. On the Assign Quota page, specify the number of Capacity Units to allocate to the SAP HANA Cloud instance within this solution. 

     

     For now, you can use the HANA Cloud estimator from SAP Discovery Centre. 
    https://hcsizingestimator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/hana/estimator/index.html 
    The BDC Calculator does not yet include SAP HANA Cloud. When estimating HANA Cloud capacity within BDC, please run the following interim approach: run the HANA Cloud estimator and multiply the result by 0.836 to calculate the equivalent BDC Capacity Units. This short-term workaround will be replaced soon.

  4. The Set Up Systems step configures the underlying system integrations required for your HANA Cloud solution. Two integrations must be addressed:

    • Integration with SAP HANA Cloud — It is set to default as add a new system to let the wizard provision a fresh HANA Cloud system on your behalf. This is the recommended approach for new deployments.
    • Integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services — Choose the CIS tenant that will handle identity and authentication for this solution (e.g. the CIS tenant that is configured with your BTP Cockpit).

       

  5. Input the details in the Configure Parameters. Define the instance-level parameters for SAP HANA Cloud. These settings control the infrastructure characteristics of the HANA database that will be provisioned. Select the Region, give an instance name and select the preset options.
    See also Data Center Availability in SAP Business Data Cloud.

  6. Formations define how BDC integrates the provisioned systems into a cohesive, connected landscape. On this page, configure the formation that links your HANA Cloud instance to the BDC solution. The Formation Type is pre-populated based on the selected BDC application. The Formation Name is a unique identifier within your landscape — choose a descriptive name that reflects the solution's purpose or environment.

  7. The final Review page presents a consolidated summary of all configuration choices made across the previous five steps. Verify each section carefully:

    • 1. General Information — Entitlement System, Solution Name, Path, Business Type.
    • 2. Assign Quota — Capacity Units allocated to SAP HANA Cloud.
    • 3. Set Up Systems — SAP HANA Cloud system and CIS tenant selection.
    • 4. Configure Parameters — Region, Instance Name, Preset.
    • 5. Set Up Formations — Formation Type and Formation Name.

    Each section includes an Edit link for inline corrections without losing the rest of the configuration. Once satisfied, click Finish to submit the provisioning request. The HANA Cloud instance will begin provisioning in the background.


  8. A confirmation message notifies you that the system is now creating the solution. Choose View in Resources to view the tenant in the resource group you have indicated


  9. While the solution is being created, the status in the Resources tab is Processing, and it will reach Ready status when provisioning is complete.



  10. Once the tenant is ready, you will be able to access the tenant from the link under the ‘Resource’ tab and under the tenant details
  11. Under ‘Customer Landscape’, you can see the system details of the new tenant. Also, the system integration is visually presented, making it easier to understand and navigate through your system landscape
  12. As a next step, you will have to add the tenant to the ‘Formation’ that you have created already and add your BDC tenant to it
  13. Navigate to me.sap.com > Products & portfolio > Business Data Cloud > Customer Landscape > Formation
  14. Choose the correct Formation and then click on ‘Include System’ and choose the Databricks tenant to include in the formation, and save the changes
  15. You can add one or more HANA Cloud systems in a single formation. You can also add the same system in multiple formations

Next Steps:

The SAP HANA Cloud instance is provisioned with a DBADMIN user with a generated password.

  • Log in to the IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant you selected for integration with the instance. Assign the “HANA Cloud Security Administrator” role to a user. This user will then be able to configure the DBADMIN account using SAP HANA Cloud tools. For additional details, refer to Managing SAP HANA Database Instances Using SAP HANA Cloud Central.

 Log in to the HANA Cloud Central and configure the DBADMIN user

  • As a next step, log in to the SAP HANA Database Explorer 


Additional Information:

After integrating an SAP Business Data Cloud system with SAP HANA Cloud, you need to share the required data products with the SAP HANA Cloud system through the catalogue (see Sharing Data Products to SAP Systems).

In SAP HANA Cloud, you must then perform additional steps to install the shared data products. For guidance, refer to Consuming Data Products in SAP HANA Cloud in the SAP HANA Cloud documentation.

Now, you have successfully provisioned SAP HANA Cloud within the BDC Core.

Happy Learning

Regards,
Shreyasri Bose

entry.title: 📣 Big News for SAP HANA Pros - The Practical Certification Era is Here 📣
entry.content:
SAP is moving away from traditional multiple-choice questions toward performance-based, system-based practical exams. If you are looking to validate your skills as a Database Administrator, it’s no longer just about what you know, it’s about what you can do in a live environment.

The SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA exam is now available in this new format. This means you'll be tasked with performing real-world administrative duties like deploying databases, configuration, backup, and setting up HA scenarios in an SAP HANA Cloud system.

🛠️ How to Upskill & Get Certified
Here are the essential resources to get you started:
▪️Prepare with the Learning Journey: Becoming a Certified Database Administrator - SAP HANA
▪️Take the Exam: SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA

💡What is "SAP Certification Reimagined"?
SAP is transforming its entire certification portfolio to reflect the modern AI era. The Certification Reimagined initiative introduces:
▪️System-Based Exams: Hands-on tasks in a live, time-boxed SAP environment.
▪️Scenario-Based Assessments: AI-driven roleplays to test your consulting and architectural advice.
▪️Open-Book Format: You can use SAP Joule, the SAP Help Portal and SAP Learning during the exam - just like you would on the job.

🔗Learn more about the transformation here: Ready to get certified with SAP?

entry.title: Database @ SAP Sapphire 2026 - Key Sessions to Attend
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire in Orlando is almost here. If you work with data platforms, analytics, or AI, this is the moment to shape an impactful agenda. Personally, I like sessions that combine vision with practical value.

For data professionals, I am recommending key SAP HANA Cloud sessions that present an in-memory database empowering all types of use cases. Each recommendation below speaks to a different part of the challenge many teams face today: how to support AI with business-ready data, how to share trusted models without unnecessary duplication, and how to turn connected data into insights people can use. I see these sessions as especially relevant for any database enthusiast.

DB for AI

The first session on my list is BDC2188, “AI Database for AI agents and apps: Overview of SAP HANA Cloud.” What makes this session mandatory is the focus on how SAP HANA Cloud supports agents and applications with in-memory, multi-model processing. I expect the session to be useful for anyone researching how AI solutions gain direct access to trusted structured and unstructured enterprise data as well as the use of AI to increase productivity.

Data Sharing

I also recommend BDC2187, “Share calculation views and data products across SAP HANA Cloud and SAP BDC.” This session gets to the heart of a practical issue many teams are working through now: how to share trusted data products and calculation views across landscapes while preserving governance and scalability. I like that the session centers on semantic models, secure data sharing, and open integration patterns - these are the capabilities that help organizations expand AI, analytics, and application development without duplicating data.

Business Context

Another session to add is BDC2632, “Semos Cloud delivers culture intelligence with AI agents on SAP HANA Cloud.” I find this one interesting because it shows how the SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine helps turn employee recognition data into a semantic layer for AI. By connecting people, skills, and behaviors, the session points to a more interactive and context-aware model for insights that goes beyond static dashboards.

HANA Cloud on the Floor

Even though these sessions are a great place to start, a  1:1 conversation with an expert can answer follow-up questions or even offer an opportunity to discuss an uncovered topic.  As you plan your time, consider stopping by the SAP HANA Cloud booth between sessions.

Before You Go

Make sure to join this Data Professionals group to connect with others on SAP HANA Cloud. Finally, consider a hands-on exploration via the SAP HANA Cloud free trial before you leave - or even while traveling to Orlando 🙂

entry.title: From Keywords to Meaning: Semantic Retrieval for SAP OData Patterns
entry.content:
From Keywords to Meaning: Semantic Retrieval for SAP OData Patterns

HANA Cloud vector search improved the matching, but exposed a harder problem: retrieval is not the same as business reasoning

SAP Community Blog  ·  May 2026  ·  12 min read  ·  Part 2 of a series

In this post

  1. Why keyword search was not enough
  2. What the pattern store actually contains
  3. Moving retrieval into HANA Cloud vector search
  4. What improved: three behaviors in practice
  5. Generating patterns from OData specs
  6. Where it failed: the "SAP SE" case
  7. What this taught me about retrieval vs reasoning
  8. What comes next

Why keyword search was not enough

Keyword search works only when users use the same words as your stored patterns. SAP users rarely do that. A procurement user may say "supplier invoice," a finance user may say "vendor invoice not yet paid," and both may expect the same underlying S/4HANA query.

In Part 1, we saw an A2A prototype on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry that mapped natural-language questions to validated SAP OData patterns and executed live queries against S/4HANA. The first retrieval layer used PostgreSQL full-text search. It worked for exact phrasing, but broke on synonyms.

So I moved the retrieval layer to HANA Cloud vector search. Semantic matching improved meaningfully in my test cases, but the more important lesson was that better retrieval does not solve business interpretation. SAP users ask in business language; S/4HANA APIs expect IDs, codes, and precise filters. Bridging that gap requires more than embeddings.

At a high level, the prototype has two flows: a runtime flow for answering user questions, and an ingestion flow for generating, enriching, and validating OData patterns.

High-level retrieval architecture for the prototype. The runtime path starts with the React / Next.js app, embeds the user query through SAP AI Core, searches the HANA Cloud pattern store using vector similarity, and executes the selected OData pattern against S/4HANA. The dashed paths show ingestion, enrichment, and possible future integration with an API catalog service.

The important design choice is that retrieval metadata, validation state, and embeddings live together in HANA Cloud. SAP AI Core handles embedding, reranking, slot extraction, and pattern generation. S/4HANA remains the source of truth for execution. The pattern store does not answer from memory; it retrieves a query shape and executes it against live APIs.

What the pattern store actually contains

Before going further, it is worth being precise about what a "pattern" is in this system. It is not a prompt, not an API template, and not a search document.

A pattern is a reusable mapping between a business question and an executable OData query shape. Each row in the ODATA_PATTERNS table stores:

  • The business meaning: description and LLM-expanded retrieval text (8-15 synonym phrasings)
  • The execution target: service name, entity set, and filter template with typed placeholders
  • The lifecycle metadata: source (curated or generated), validation status, execution count
  • The embedding: a 1536-dimensional vector used for cosine similarity at query time

Example:

Question:    "purchase orders by supplier"
Service:     API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV
Entity set:  A_PurchaseOrder
Filter:      Supplier eq '{{supplier_id}}'

At query time, the user's question is embedded and compared against every stored embedding using cosine similarity. The closest pattern wins, or goes to an LLM reranker if the score is ambiguous. If a match is found, the filter template is filled with values extracted from the question and the OData call is executed against S/4HANA. The result is live data, not generated text.

The ODATA_PATTERNS table in HANA Cloud. The 1536-dimensional embedding vector lives in the same row as the business metadata: service, entity set, filter template, validation status. No separate vector store.

Moving retrieval into HANA Cloud vector search

SAP HANA Cloud supports vector storage natively via the REAL_VECTOR data type, and cosine similarity via a built-in SQL function. The similarity computation stays inside the database engine. No rows are loaded into application memory, no separate vector service is needed:

SELECT TOP 5 ID, DESCRIPTION, FILTER_TEMPLATE, SERVICE_NAME,
  COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SCORE
FROM ODATA_PATTERNS
ORDER BY SCORE DESC

The embedding model is text-embedding-3-small via SAP AI Core, at 1536 dimensions. At write time, each pattern's description is first expanded by an LLM into 8-15 synonym phrasings before embedding. "Purchase orders by supplier" becomes "POs for vendor, orders by BP, vendor PO list, supplier purchase orders..." All of that gets embedded together into a single vector, so the embedding represents the range of business phrasings a user might realistically use, not just the original description.

What improved: three behaviors in practice

In this prototype, the reranker is a second LLM check that reviews the top retrieved candidates and either confirms the best match or rejects the query as unsupported. Queries flow through three paths depending on the cosine score: fast path (score at or above 0.65, no LLM), LLM reranker (0.45 to 0.65), or auto-reject (below 0.45).

Vocabulary mismatch: where the improvement was clearest

The clearest improvement was on vocabulary mismatch. "Vendor invoices not yet paid" matched "Show supplier invoices in the system" with a cosine score of 0.516. Only one word overlaps: invoices. Full-text search would likely have treated this as a weak match. Vector search found the semantic proximity and, after LLM reranking confirmed the match, returned live invoice data from S/4HANA.

"Vendor invoices not yet paid" matched "Show supplier invoices in the system." Only "invoices" overlaps as a keyword. Vector search found the semantic proximity that keyword search misses.

The fast path: zero LLM calls for confident matches

When the cosine score is at or above 0.65, the system accepts the match and executes without any LLM call. In this test case, "Open sales orders" scored 0.679. The curated pattern encoded the relevant S/4HANA status filter: OverallSDProcessStatus eq 'A'. The query ran, and live sales order data came back in one round trip to HANA and one to S/4HANA.

Slot extraction: filling values from the question

When a pattern has {{placeholders}}, the LLM extracts values from the question. "Purchase orders by 17401710" - the vendor ID was detected, Supplier eq '17401710' composed, and PO 4500000060 returned live from S/4HANA.

"17401710" extracted as supplier_id. Filter composed. PO 4500000060 returned from S/4HANA.

Generating patterns from OData specs

A pattern store that starts empty is not useful. Writing patterns by hand is slow: 8 curated patterns cover 3 domains. A real SAP landscape spans dozens of APIs and hundreds of relevant queries.

The OData specification files (OpenAPI/Edmx JSON committed alongside the code) contain much of what an LLM needs to propose candidate patterns: entity sets, properties, filter value enumerations, relationships. An LLM running on SAP AI Core reads a spec and proposes patterns with filter templates. One run, seven specs, 139 candidate patterns, without hand-authoring.

Seven OData specs. 140 generated candidate patterns. Business Partner, Maintenance Order, Outbound Delivery, Purchase Order, Purchase Requisition, Sales Order, Supplier Invoice.

In my test cases, generated patterns worked for common queries. "Purchase requisitions waiting for approval" matched a generated pattern using ReleaseIsNotCompleted eq true on A_PurchaseRequisitionItem, the expected field in my test system.

A generated pattern using the expected SAP status field. Real requisition data returned.

Generated patterns enter the store as candidates: SOURCE='generated', VALIDATED=FALSE. They can be retrieved and executed, but they are not promoted until confirmed.

On trust: HTTP 200 alone is not sufficient to promote a pattern. A successful response with zero records, as the next section shows, looks the same as an HTTP 200 with valid results. Promotion requires result-shape checks, expected outcome checks, repeated successful usage, or explicit human review. Execution feedback accumulates trust over time; it does not grant it immediately.

Where it failed: the "SAP SE" case

Then came the more interesting failure: "Purchase orders by SAP SE."

The slot signal detector correctly identified "SAP SE" as a supplier name after a preposition. The LLM extracted supplier_id = "SAP SE". The OData call executed, HTTP 200, and returned zero records.

Structurally correct. Semantically useless. S/4HANA filters on vendor number 17401710, not the string "SAP SE." The execution completes with HTTP 200 and returns nothing. No error. No signal that anything went wrong.

This is the failure mode that most AI demonstrations avoid showing. There is no error, no stack trace. The system did exactly what it was designed to do and returned a response indistinguishable from "no matching purchase orders exist," when the real situation was that the query was structurally correct but semantically wrong.

This is not an edge case in SAP environments. Users think in supplier names, customer names, plant names, and material descriptions. The system identifiers (vendor number, plant code, material group) are database keys that users encounter only incidentally. For genuinely unsupported questions, the reranker returns a clean rejection; that part worked. The harder problem was when the system returned a technically successful but semantically empty result.

What this taught me about retrieval vs reasoning

Semantic retrieval solves matching. It does not solve interpretation. In SAP, users ask in business language; the APIs work in system identifiers. Closing that gap, reliably, at scale, requires something beyond embeddings.

Vector search genuinely improved vocabulary mismatch: "vendor invoices not yet paid" finding "Show supplier invoices," "Open" resolving through a curated status-code pattern. These hold up in practice.

What vector search does not address is the gap between business language and system identifiers. When a user says "SAP SE," the retrieval system correctly identifies the intent (supplier filter) and correctly identifies the slot (supplier_id). The embedding did its job. The failure is downstream: filling a system identifier slot with a human-readable name. No similarity threshold, no synonym expansion, no reranker fixes this. It requires a lookup, a small MDM problem embedded inside a retrieval problem.

What this implies about trust is equally important: a pattern can be structurally correct and semantically wrong at the same time. HTTP 200 is not a signal of correctness; it is a signal of execution. A zero-record result from a name-based filter is indistinguishable from a zero-record result from a legitimate empty query. The trust pipeline has to account for this, which means execution feedback alone is insufficient as a validation signal.

How patterns earn trust: the full lifecycle

  1. OData spec to LLM generator: reads entity sets and fields, proposes candidate patterns with filter templates
  2. Candidate pattern: enters store as SOURCE=generated, VALIDATED=FALSE
  3. Validation: field checks, allowlist checks, result-shape checks
  4. S/4HANA execution: result-shape and expected outcome checks; pass promotes the pattern
  5. VALIDATED=TRUE: execution count accumulates; repeated failure flags the pattern for review

Trust accumulates through use, not assumption. Promotion requires result-shape checks, repeated successful usage, or human review. HTTP 200 alone is not enough.

What comes next

Three open problems remain within the pattern store model, becoming more acute as the corpus grows.

Score calibration at scale. The 0.65 threshold was calibrated on 139 patterns where the right answer is almost always the top result. With 5,000 patterns across 50 domains, the embedding space becomes denser. Adjacent domain vocabulary competes, the reranker fires more often, and latency compounds. Domain pre-filtering before the cosine comparison (WHERE SERVICE_NAME = ?) is the structural fix, but requires knowing the domain before retrieval.

Slot values as system identifiers. "SAP SE" is the visible case, but the same gap exists for plant codes, material group codes, customer numbers, and cost center codes. Every slot that takes a master data value has this problem. Closing it requires a lookup layer between slot detection and slot filling.

Single-shot retrieval has a reasoning ceiling. The pattern store maps one question to one pattern. That covers a large share of transactional queries. But some questions require chaining: look up a supplier, then find their open POs; find the goods receipt for this invoice; show everything in this Procure-to-Pay process. These cannot be answered by retrieving a single pattern.

Part 3: retrieval based on API semantics

Part 2 improved retrieval inside a pattern-store model. Part 3 moves closer to the A2A goal: API-semantic retrieval, runtime tool loops, and multi-step SAP business capability execution.

Spoiler
Running alongside the pattern store, I explored a different retrieval model, one that embeds entire API specifications rather than individual query patterns, and uses an LLM tool loop to compose queries at runtime rather than filling pre-validated templates. That approach handled "purchase orders by SAP SE" more naturally, because the LLM could chain a supplier lookup before the purchase order query. But it surfaces different questions about trust, scale, and how you validate dynamically generated OData calls. Part 3 covers how that system works, where it scales, and where it does not.

This post reflects a personal learning exercise built on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with HANA Cloud and SAP AI Core. All views are my own and do not represent SAP's positions, roadmap, or product direction. Code snippets are from personal prototypes and are shared for illustrative purposes only.

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter May 2026
entry.content:
Dear customers,
Find here a preview of the SAP Enterprise Support Academy monthly newsletter on latest news, training courses, upcoming sessions, and further educational offerings.
The SAP Enterprise Support Academy provides expert guidance and high impact knowledge transfer services that enable you to innovate and be successful with your SAP solutions.

Subscribe to regularly receive latest news and stay informed about the wide range of educational offerings provided by the SAP Enterprise Support Academy that help you to build the digital skills tailored to your needs.

SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-guided Implementation Workshop Series

Unlock real-time insights with SAP HANA Cloud and fast-track your transformation. Discover our Expert-guided Implementation workshops to master analytics, AI, and multi-model data—helping you build smarter, faster, data-driven applications on SAP BTP.

Read the full blog

Compliance Easier with SAP Ariba Supplier Management

Curious how supplier compliance can feel less like constant follow‑up and more like a steady, structured process? This article highlights the simple shifts that help keep supplier data current, risks visible, and everyday collaboration running without friction.

Learn more

Moving SAP Ariba Data from On‑Premise to Cloud

Planning a move from SAP Ariba On‑Premise to the cloud? This FAQ explains what data migrates automatically, what needs manual handling, and how SAP recommends managing history.

Learn more and get started

What Is SAP Enterprise Service Management and Why It Matters Now

An employee waits on HR, a supplier chases billing, and delays build. See how SAP Enterprise Service Management connects service across teams to speed outcomes and simplify work.

Learn more

Strengthen Your SAP Projects in Q2

Join the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC) for hands-on SAP support, early access to new services, and influence on future offerings. New workstreams for Q2: CQC Transformation Assessments for SAP Transportation Management and SAP Extended Warehouse Management. Five workstreams are still open to new customers in the BTM, BTP, and Cloud ERP areas.

Full details

SAP Services & Support Sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 (Orlando & Madrid)

Explore the full breadth of the evolving SAP Services & Support portfolio- from foundational guidance to advanced and Max-success plans - alongside portfolio extensions such as development services, application management, and SAP Sovereign Cloud, designed to help you accelerate innovation and unlock greater business value.

Orlando sessions Services and Support
Madrid sessions Services and Support

Support Accreditation – Don’t Miss Out on SAP Support You Already Have

Getting Started with Support from SAP is a free, self‑paced course on how to truly get value from SAP support — and many are surprised by how much they’ve been missing. The course demystifies SAP for Me and walks through powerful self‑service tools, live support channels, AI‑guided support, and SAP Cloud ALM. With just a few short units and a digital record of achievement at the end, Support Accreditation is one of the easiest ways to unlock better support.

Get started here

New Ways to Drive Customer Value with SAP Enterprise Support — Watch the Replay

Catch the replay of our March 25 session to discover what’s new in SAP Enterprise Support and how you can unlock even more value from your SAP investments.

In this session, we covered:

  • The move of SAP Enterprise Support Academy to learning.sap.com, including expert‑led guidance and learning opportunities
  • SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council workstreams you can join today

Watch the replay

Guided Walk‑thru for the What’s New Viewer

Take a quick, interactive tour to discover what’s new in your upcoming release and understand what’s changing before you upgrade.

Start the guided walkthrough

Personalized SAP Enterprise Support Guidance in SAP for Me

A new SAP for Me feature brings value map knowledge base resources together in one place, delivering personalized learning recommendations based on your purchased products.

Start exploring

Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando (ASUG Pre-Conference, May 11-2026)

Join an exclusive ASUG pre-conference with SAP leaders to explore the new Services & Support portfolio, maximize your cloud subscription, and gain VIP access to SAP’s Experience Center.

  • Seats are limited — register now 
  • More information here

Which Integration Scenario Are You In? 3 SAP BTP Guides to Help You Move Forward

Explore the blog post to discover key Integration knowledge-based articles from the SAP BTP Value Map. Whether you are building integration expertise, planning your migration from SAP Neo, or transitioning from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite, this blog provides guidance and curated resources to support your journey. Discover how to navigate your integration landscape with confidence and take the next step toward a modern, cloud-based architecture.

Learn more

Help Shape the Future of SAP Products

Join the new SAP User Research Panel to participate in brief research studies and help improve SAP products and solutions. After registering, you will receive an invitation when a study matches your profile. Each study typically takes 30–60 minutes and can be completed remotely through usability tests, surveys, or interviews.

Join here

Performance Meets Innovation at SAP Sapphire 2026! SAP is thrilled to welcome two-time Formula 1 World Champion Mika Häkkinen as a special guest in Orlando and Madrid. Join us at the Services and Support Center to see how the mindset of a world champion—speed, resilience, teamwork, and precision—translates into business transformation. entry.title: The Flyweel is turning: Partners leverage SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP BTP during Hack2Build
entry.content:
In the fast-paced digital landscape of 2025, SAP unveiled its plans for SAP Business Data Cloud as well as SAP HANA Cloud at SAP TechEd 2025, positioning itself as a leading force in data-driven innovation. Building on these announcements, SAP’s latest Hack2Build event explored the full potential of building applications on SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Hack2Build on Building Applications on SAP Business Data Cloud provided an immersive environment for partners to leverage the advanced capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud as well as HANA Cloud and BTP, thus tapping even into the realm of agentic AI.  Equipped with hands-on coaching from SAP experts, participating partners became frontrunners in leveraging cutting-edge technologies, empowering them to transform insights into actionable solutions.

During the Hack2Build initiative, partners engaged in a seven-day hack phase, constructing functional applications that harnessed the power of real-time data analytics, advanced AI-driven business intelligence and actionable insights. The event emphasized seamless integration with SAP and non-SAP systems, and the capability to translate raw data into analytical insights and from there into strategic actions.

The culmination of the event featured a Live Demo Jam, where partners presented their innovative prototypes to a panel of SAP judges. This collaborative event showcased the ingenuity and potential of SAP's latest technology advancements, paving the way for future applications that bridge the gap between data insights and actionable business strategies.

It was again amazing to see how partners turned their innovative solution visions into running prototypes within seven days and delivering even full breakthroughs from sourcing the data through analytical modeling into leveraging the insights in Joule agents. The flywheel is truly turning now!

Participating Partners:

Participating Partners in the Hack2Build Building Applications on BDC, Apr 22-29 2026

🏆Winners

With that momentum in motion, the judges selected three solutions that stood out across technical completeness, workflow clarity, and customer relevance.

🥇NTT Data Business Solutions 

Manufacturers often face unexpected disruptions that reduce operational efficiency and can delay customer deliveries. The Intelligent Manufacturing solution addresses this by using SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform to combine data from SAP ERP, SAP Data Products, custom Data Products, machine telemetry, and energy consumption. These data sources are used to train AI models in SAP Databricks, enabling early detection of production orders at risk due to supplier delays, plant failures, equipment issues, lack of resources, or other factors. The solution provides actionable insights through SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Build, while SAP Build Process Automation triggers real-time notifications for risk mitigation.

 

🥈cbs Consulting

cbs solution AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization aims at transforming supply chain resilience using SAP BDC, BTP, and AI. By integrating SAP S/4HANA and SAP IBP data with external market drivers (Oil prices, Forex Rates, & Trade Routes) via SAP Datasphere, the data is harmonized, while predictive models from SAP Databricks assess demand variability, supply risk, and external factors to generate optimal stock recommendations. SAP Analytics Cloud provides simulations, insights, and planning capabilities, while SAP Build Apps triggers the multi-level approval workflow and operationalise decisions back into core systems (SAP S/4HANA). The result? A closed-loop, intelligent process that reduces excess inventory and boosts market responsiveness.

 

🥉SDG Group

In oil and gas, an unplanned equipment failure costs $45,000–$60,000 per hour. The manual response cycle stretches that loss across 48 hours on average. Rig-Guard consolidates historical NPT records and live sensor readings (temperature, pressure, vibration) into a single source of truth on SAP BDC, then runs a predictive ML model on SAP Databricks to catch failures before they happen. When a prediction fires, an agentic workflow on SAP Build Process Automation takes it from insight to action: Autonomous above 95% confidence, Human in the Loop below - failure caught, revenue protected. The rig watches itself. The rig never stops.

 

⭐ Finalists

All other finalists proved themselves to be winners in their own right through the enthusiasm and creativity they demonstrated, underscoring the vitality of SAP's partner ecosystem. Across industries such as finance, supply chain, retail, and more, these accomplished partners delivered BDC-based solutions with clear business relevance and strong potential for rapid customer adoption. Their impressive prototypes showcased how Applications, Data & AI come to life and truly turn the flywheel.

 

Partner Company

Solution Title

All for One

AI Planning Insight Agent for SAP Analytics Cloud & Datasphere

cbs

AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization

Deloitte

CFO Impact Analyzer

DXC

SAP Customer Delivery Preference Identification

EY

MarketIQ

IBM

Revenue Timing & Value Conflicts with Tariff Resolution

Infosys

Infosys Size Curve Prediction Solution for Retail

KPMG

AI Copilot for Real-Time Supplier Risk & Sourcing Decisions

NTT DATA Business Solutions

Manufacturing Intelligence

PWC

24/7 Reconciliation & Monitoring

 

What’s Next

All participating partners now move into a six‑week Build Phase, where they will refine and extend their prototypes with support from SAP product management, Partner Ecosystem Success as well as many other experts in BDC, BTP and AI. This phase will focus on preparing solutions for customer validation and potential go‑to‑market discussions.

Acknowledgments

Many thanks to all partner teams, Judges, Databricks team and SAP contributors across Business Data Cloud Product Management, Customer Success & Delivery, Value Prototyping, BTP, BDC and AI Solution Architects, Partner Excellence Center Leads and Partner Ecosystem Success for making this edition of Hack2Build a milestone in spinning the flywheel of Applications, Data & AI!

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026 - AI database for AI agents and apps: Overview of SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community!

My name is Daniel Dukes, and I’m on SAP’s Technical Product Marketing team. My primary focus is SAP HANA Cloud. Day to day, I spend a lot of time evangelizing the practicality of our database platform at events and through content published in our Data Professionals community. I’ve been in the SAP ecosystem for close to 25 years with the last 15 years at SAP. Much of my tenure has involved working with in-memory data technology in one form or another – from the SAP BW Accelerator to SAP HANA Cloud. 

Database @ SAP Sapphire 2026 in Orlando

I am really looking forward to this year in Orlando. Here is a “sneak peek” of my session at 1:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12 - AI database for AI agents and apps: Overview of SAP HANA Cloud | BDC2188.

The current plan is to walk through the AI-related features in SAP HANA Cloud from two angles. The first topic covers how HANA Cloud can enable AI scenarios - e.g., as the data layer for agents and generative AI services. I also plan to review how HANA Cloud uses AI to improve developer and user productivity. Along with an overview of the solution, I also want to discuss the kinds of use cases this database can empower.

Additionally, here are some other highly recommended SAP HANA Cloud sessions: 

  • Share calculation views and data products across SAP HANA Cloud and SAP BDC | BDC2187 @ 2:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12
  • Build smarter AI agents with an agent memory service in SAP HANA Cloud | BDC 7224 @ 3:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12
  • Semos Cloud delivers culture intelligence with AI agents on SAP HANA Cloud | BDC2632 @ 2:30 PM EDT on Wednesday, May 13
  • SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2947 @ Demo Station

Discover, Connect, Learn, and Grow

If you are ready to explore SAP HANA Cloud beyond these experiences, the quickest hands-on starting point is the SAP HANA Cloud basic trial. It’s a straightforward way to try features yourself without needing a full landscape. After that, I’d suggest working through the hands-on tutorials within the Discovery Center. A third great recommendation is to join other Data Professionals in the SAP Community. Feel free to participate in a place built for those who work with data.

Share Your Thoughts and Experiences

Until we meet in person, let’s correspond through the SAP community. Consider posting a reply to the following question. I’d appreciate any concrete examples (even small ones) shared in the comments section. 

Prompt: Where do you see AI adding real value in your data platform work today?

~

Looking forward to meeting you at my session or on the show floor 😊

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA
entry.content:
A lot of SAP professionals learn database administration while juggling project deadlines, system issues, and last-minute troubleshooting.  Honestly, that’s how many of the best lessons happen.

But having structured guidance changes the pace completely.

Our new Partner Learning Accelerator for the SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA certification was created to help partners prepare with more clarity, more practice, and fewer surprises on exam day.

Across two live sessions, we’ll walk through topics that database administrators deal with every day:

  • Monitoring and configuration
